name: Invoice grand total off by one cent on multi-currency orders
task: Users report that invoice grand totals are occasionally off by one cent compared to what the line items should sum to, but ONLY for orders that mix currencies (e.g. a EUR-priced item settled onto a USD invoice). Single-currency orders are always correct. Starting from the invoice API handler, find the true root cause of this discrepancy and propose the minimal correct fix. Do not guess from the entry file alone.
description: HIDDEN GROUND TRUTH. Root cause file: test-project-large/util/money.py, function quantize_minor. The defect is a rounding-policy mismatch at a single boundary. The module docstring of util/money.py states the group accounting standard is banker's rounding (ROUND_HALF_EVEN), and the sibling function to_minor() correctly uses ROUND_HALF_EVEN. But quantize_minor()'s signature is `def quantize_minor(amount, currency, rounding=ROUND_HALF_UP)` -- its DEFAULT rounding argument is ROUND_HALF_UP, contradicting the documented standard. quantize_minor is the single settlement choke point that both domain/pricing.py (PricingEngine._settle_net, which settles each converted+discounted line net) and domain/tax.py (tax_on_net) flow through, and both call it WITHOUT passing an explicit rounding= argument, so they silently get HALF_UP. For single-currency orders the converted amount never lands on an exact half-cent, so HALF_UP and HALF_EVEN agree and totals are correct. For multi-currency orders, currency conversion (domain/currency.py multiplying by an FX rate from infra/rates.py) can produce an amount that lands EXACTLY on a half-cent (e.g. CatalogItem SKU-GADGET = EUR 14.50, quantity 2 => 29.00 EUR, converted to USD at rate EUR->USD = 1.0850 => exactly 31.465000 USD). quantize_minor rounds 31.465 up to 31.47 (HALF_UP) where the documented banker's standard rounds to 31.46 (HALF_EVEN, because 6 is even). That single line is then one cent too high; services/invoice_builder.py sums the settled line grosses into grand_total_minor, so the grand total is one cent higher than the correct banker's-rounded figure. CONCRETE REPRODUCTION: order with lines [SKU-GADGET x2 (EUR), SKU-WIDGET x1 (USD)], target_currency USD, US customer, no discount => system grand_total = 5519 minor units, correct banker's-rounded grand_total = 5518. The GADGET line net settles to 3147 instead of 3146. MINIMAL CORRECT FIX: change the default in util/money.py quantize_minor from `rounding=ROUND_HALF_UP` to `rounding=ROUND_HALF_EVEN`, so the single settlement boundary matches the documented group standard and matches to_minor(). No other file needs to change; pricing.py and tax.py already route through quantize_minor and rely on its default. NOTE: the symptom file api/invoices.py contains NO arithmetic and NO defect -- its own docstring correctly states both grand_total and line gross come straight from the assembled invoice, so the discrepancy must originate upstream.
expected_actions: Open the symptom entry file api/invoices.py and confirm it performs no money arithmetic (grand_total and line gross are passed through from the builder).

Follow the import/call chain: api/invoices.py -> services/invoice_builder.py (InvoiceBuilder.build) -> domain/pricing.py (PricingEngine.price_line/_settle_net) -> domain/tax.py (tax_on_net) -> util/money.py (quantize_minor).
Recognize that the per-currency aspect points at currency conversion (domain/currency.py + infra/rates.py) feeding fractional amounts into the settlement boundary.
Reach util/money.py and read quantize_minor; notice its default rounding is ROUND_HALF_UP while the module docstring and to_minor() use ROUND_HALF_EVEN (the stated group standard).
Identify the single-boundary half-up vs banker's-rounding mismatch as the root cause: callers in pricing.py and tax.py do not pass rounding= and silently inherit HALF_UP.

Explain why it is multi-currency only: conversion can produce an exact .xx5 half-cent (e.g. EUR 29.00 x 1.0850 = 31.465 USD) where HALF_UP and HALF_EVEN diverge; same-currency lines never hit the half-cent.
Propose the minimal fix: change quantize_minor's default rounding to ROUND_HALF_EVEN; confirm no caller relied on HALF_UP.

Do NOT propose changes localized to api/invoices.py, services/invoice_builder.py, or a sum-then-round restructuring of the builder -- those are wrong/incomplete; the defect is the rounding mode at the util boundary, not the summation order.

name: Which module is the safe first extraction vs the coupling hub to avoid
task: The team wants to begin splitting this backend into separately deployable packages. Without breaking anything, identify (a) the single safest module to extract first -- a genuine pure leaf with no inbound coupling from business logic and no project-internal imports -- and (b) the highest-coupling module that should NOT be the first cut because too many modules depend on it. Justify both answers by mapping the dependency graph across files, not by reading any one file.
description: HIDDEN GROUND TRUTH. (a) Safest first extraction / purest leaf: util/ids.py (and util/clock.py and util/errors.py are also pure leaves). util/ids.py imports only the Python stdlib (itertools, time) and nothing from the project; it is imported by other layers but depends on no project module, so extracting it cannot create a cycle or pull in domain logic. util/clock.py (stdlib datetime only) and util/errors.py (no imports at all) are equally pure leaves. Among these, util/ids.py / util/clock.py / util/errors.py are all valid 'safe first cut' answers. The BEST single answer is util/errors.py or util/ids.py: zero project imports, leaf in the graph. (b) Highest-coupling hub to AVOID cutting first: util/money.py. In the current tree it is imported by 7 modules spanning all three upper layers -- domain/tax.py, domain/pricing.py, domain/fees.py, api/serializers.py, services/invoice_builder.py, services/payment_service.py, services/reporting_service.py -- and it is the SSOT for the rounding policy, so it carries behavior, not just data. Extracting or altering it first risks the widest blast radius (and is exactly where the scenario-01 bug lives). The data hub domain/models.py has the highest raw fan-in (imported by ~11 modules: catalog, pricing, repository, customer_service, inventory_service, invoice_builder, order_service, payment_service, reporting_service, serializers, app) but is pure dataclasses. A correct answer names util/money.py as the behavioral hub to avoid (strongest, because changing it changes computed money everywhere) and/or domain/models.py as the structural hub. CORRECT METHOD: build the import graph by reading only the import lines of all modules; count inbound edges; a node whose out-degree to project modules is 0 is the safe cut, a node with high in-degree is the hub.
expected_actions: Enumerate every module's project-internal imports across api/, services/, domain/, infra/, util/ (read import lines, not full files).
Build the dependency graph and compute in-degree (how many project modules import each) and out-degree to project modules.
Identify the pure leaves with zero project imports: util/ids.py, util/clock.py, util/errors.py.
Name a pure leaf (util/ids.py or util/errors.py) as the safe first extraction and justify: no project imports => extracting cannot create a cycle.

Identify the high in-degree hub: util/money.py (imported by tax, pricing, invoice_builder, serializers) and/or domain/models.py (imported by catalog, pricing, repository, invoice_builder, serializers).
Name util/money.py (carries the shared rounding policy) as the hub to avoid cutting first and justify by its wide inbound coupling and behavioral centrality.
